profile.title: Snippets library
profile.subtitle: Reusable text, email phrases, and attachments you can insert into any application email.

cv.content:
Academic CV package for PhD applications and outreach.

Suggested sections:
• Education (degrees, GPA, ranking if strong)
• Research experience (role, methods, outcomes)
• Publications & preprints
• Awards, scholarships, competitions
• Skills (languages, tools, lab techniques)
• Service / teaching (if relevant)

Tips: keep 1–3 pages; put the strongest research first; attach the latest PDF version.

personalStatement.content:
Personal statement — story, motivation, and program fit.

Outline:
1. Opening hook (what drives your research interest)
2. Academic / research path that prepared you
3. Key projects and what you learned
4. Why this field / why now
5. Why this program / group (specific fit)
6. Career goals after the PhD

Notes: avoid generic praise; name labs, courses, or papers that genuinely connect to your plan.

sop.content:
Statement of purpose (formal portal version).

Outline:
1. Research interests (precise, not vague)
2. Preparation (coursework, projects, skills)
3. Evidence of research ability
4. Fit with program faculty and resources
5. Contribution you hope to make
6. Closing with clear next step

Keep a short “core paragraph” you can reuse across portals, then customize the fit section per school.

researchProposal.content:
Research proposal / project plan.

Suggested structure:
• Title & one-sentence summary
• Background & gap in the literature
• Research questions / hypotheses
• Methods, data, or experimental design
• Timeline (year 1–3 or milestones)
• Expected contributions
• Fit with host lab / supervisor expertise
• Resources needed (compute, access, equipment)

Attach figures, related work notes, or a short abstract as files if helpful.

researchStatement.content:
Research statement — past work and future agenda.

Outline:
1. Research identity (themes that connect your work)
2. Past projects (problem → approach → result → your role)
3. Current directions
4. Future 3–5 year agenda
5. Collaboration style and independence

Use this for faculty applications, some fellowships, and as a long-form companion to a short proposal.

teachingStatement.content:
Teaching statement.

Outline:
• Teaching philosophy (how students learn in your field)
• Experience (TA, tutoring, workshops, mentoring)
• Concrete classroom or lab examples
• Assessment and feedback approach
• Inclusive teaching practices
• Courses you could teach or develop

Even if teaching is secondary for a research PhD, a short version helps for TA roles and some packages.

coverLetter.content:
Cover letter / professor outreach (cold email).

Template blocks:
• Subject line (clear + specific)
• 1–2 sentence self-intro (degree, school, interest)
• Why this professor’s work (cite 1–2 papers/projects)
• What you bring (skills, results, relevant experience)
• Ask (meeting, RA opportunity, application advice)
• Attachments list (CV, transcript, paper)
• Polite close + contact details

Keep under ~200–250 words; personalize every professor.

transcript.content:
Transcripts and academic records.

Track:
• Official vs unofficial transcript
• GPA (scale + conversion if needed)
• Ranking / class standing (if available)
• Key coursework for your research area
• Degree certificates / enrollment letters
• Language of document + certified translation

Upload the newest PDF; note any pending grades in private notes.

languageScores.content:
Language and standardized test scores.

Record for each test:
• Test name (TOEFL / IELTS / Duolingo / GRE / …)
• Date taken & validity window
• Overall + section scores
• Score report PDF / verification code
• School minimum requirements vs your scores

Useful for portal forms and for mentioning readiness in emails.

recommendation.content:
Recommendation letters tracker.

For each recommender:
• Name, title, institution, email
• Relationship (how long, which projects)
• Letter type (portal / email / sealed PDF)
• Deadline & status (asked / accepted / submitted)
• Materials shared with them (CV, draft SOP, bullet points)
• Thank-you / reminder log

Attach templates of talking points you send to recommenders.

writingSample.content:
Writing sample package.

Include:
• Title, venue/course, year
• Length / format required by program
• Abstract or short summary
• Your contribution (if co-authored)
• PDF of the polished sample
• Optional: shorter excerpt if portals have page limits

Prefer work that shows research reasoning, not only polished prose.

publications.content:
Publication and output list.

Organize by type:
• Journal / conference papers (accepted, under review, preprint)
• Workshops, posters, demos
• Patents, open-source releases, datasets
• Citation format (consistent style)
• Links (DOI, arXiv, GitHub)
• Authorship order and your role

Keep a “highlights” short list for emails and a full list for CV.

portfolio.content:
Portfolio of projects and demos.

For each project:
• One-line problem statement
• Your role and timeline
• Methods / stack
• Outcome (metric, demo, users, paper)
• Link (site, video, repo) + screenshot if useful
• What you want readers to notice

Great for HCI, systems, ML demos, design, and interdisciplinary work.

scholarshipEssay.content:
Scholarship / fellowship essay.

Common prompts to draft against:
• Academic goals and research plan
• Leadership, service, or impact
• Financial need or opportunity gap (if asked)
• Why this award advances your PhD path
• Concrete use of funds

Maintain a master draft, then cut to each funder’s word limit. Attach scoring rubrics if you track them.

other.content: Custom reusable material, phrase, link, or attachment bundle for applications and outreach.

profile.globalPhraseHint: Applies whenever you insert one or more snippets into an email draft — compose it as {{A}} + name(s) + {{B}}.
profile.phraseSuffixPlaceholderZh: 例如：草稿，方便您了解我的申请背景。
profile.notesPlaceholder: Notes only you can see — never inserted into an email.
profile.reserveUploadHint: Keep this snippet ready for a later upload or shared upload.
profile.uploadReservedHint: Upload slot reserved. Add files yourself later or share an upload link.
profile.requestUploadHint: Share a link so someone else can upload a file straight into this snippet.
profile.selectSnippets: Select one or more snippets to insert
profile.noSnippetsToInsert: No snippets yet — add one from the Profile screen.
profile.aiProfileReadyHint: Ready to allow for an AI draft when you choose.
profile.aiProfileDisclosure: This profile is private by default. It is sent only when you actively enable it for an AI draft.

aiProfileFields.boundaries: Claims to avoid or sensitive context

aiProfilePlaceholders.preferredName: How you would like to be addressed
aiProfilePlaceholders.currentRole: e.g. Research assistant or master's student
aiProfilePlaceholders.researchInterests: Use one or two sentences for the questions and fields you want to pursue
aiProfilePlaceholders.researchMethods: Methods, tools, datasets, or approaches you know
aiProfilePlaceholders.achievements: Projects, papers, awards, or practice worth mentioning
aiProfilePlaceholders.goals: Use one or two sentences for your PhD goal and contribution
aiProfilePlaceholders.writingTone: e.g. concise, warm, and academically direct
aiProfilePlaceholders.boundaries: Anything AI should not claim, disclose, or overstate
